name: WebP
description: WebP is a modern image format developed by Google that provides superior lossless and lossy compression for images on the web. Using WebP, webmasters and web developers can create smaller, richer images that make the web faster.
pros: 26% smaller in size compared to PNGs
cons: Older systems (like old macOS versions) may struggle to natively preview them without a browser
browserSupport: Supported by 96%+ of all global web browsers (Chrome, Safari, Firefox, Edge).

name: AVIF
description: AVIF is an ultra-modern, open-source image format based on the AV1 video codec. It offers significantly better compression than WebP, JPEG, and PNG, resulting in incredibly small file sizes without noticeable quality loss. It is considered the 'next generation' of web images.
pros: Currently the most efficient compression algorithm available
Supports HDR and wide color gamuts
cons: Encoding can be CPU-intensive and slow
Not fully supported on older browsers like Internet Explorer or old Safari

name: PNG
description: PNG is an unpatented raster image format that supports lossless data compression. It was created as an improved, non-patented replacement for Graphics Interchange Format (GIF) and is the most widely used lossless image compression format on the Internet.
useCases: Images with text where sharpness is critical
pros: Universal compatibility across all devices and software
cons: File sizes can become massive for complex photographs
Does not support native animation (requires APNG)

name: JPEG
description: JPEG is a commonly used method of lossy compression for digital images, particularly for those images produced by digital photography. The degree of compression can be adjusted, allowing a selectable tradeoff between storage size and image quality.
pros: Universally recognized and readable by every device
cons: Lossy compression means quality permanently degrades upon every save (generation loss)

name: HEIC
description: HEIC is Apple's proprietary version of the HEIF format. Introduced in iOS 11, it uses High-Efficiency Video Coding (HEVC) to compress images to half the size of a standard JPEG while maintaining the same visual quality. It is the default photo format for iPhones.
pros: Half the file size of a JPEG with better quality
Can store multiple images in one file (Live Photos)
cons: Proprietary format not natively supported by Windows or standard web browsers
Requires conversion before uploading to most websites
browserSupport: 0% native browser support (requires conversion to JPEG/WebP for web use).

name: SVG
description: SVG is an XML-based vector image format for two-dimensional graphics. Unlike raster formats (JPEG, PNG) that use a grid of pixels, SVG uses mathematical equations to draw shapes. This means SVG images can be scaled to any size without losing quality.
pros: Can be styled with CSS and animated with JavaScript
cons: Cannot be used for complex photographs
Complex SVGs with thousands of paths can slow down browser rendering
